cues.x: Building a StoryBrand, by Donald Miller.
Let me start with a confession that Donald Miller makes early in this book, because it is the crack in the wall that the whole thing pours through.
He built a business.
He had a genuinely good product, something people needed, something that worked.
And it was quietly failing.

Not because the product was bad, but because when people asked him what he did, he would open his mouth and out would come a tangle of words, a description that impressed him and confused everyone else.
He had a message nobody could repeat.
And a message nobody can repeat is a message that dies in the room it was spoken in.
It never travels.
It never gets forwarded.
It never gets said again at a dinner table or in a meeting when you are not there to say it yourself.
He realized that he had spent years thinking his problem was the product, or the pricing, or the marketing budget, when his real problem was that he was confusing people.
And confused people do not buy.
They just leave.
That is the book in a seed.

Building a StoryBrand is about one skill, and it is arguably the most valuable skill in all of business, which is the ability to talk about what you do in a way that other people actually understand and care about.
Miller runs a company called StoryBrand, and he came to marketing not through business school but through storytelling.
He was a memoirist, a writer, someone who spent years learning why some stories grip you by the collar and others let you drift off to sleep.
And at some point he had a realization that reorganized his entire career.

The same rules that make a movie work, that keep you in your seat for two hours caring about whether a fictional person gets what they want, those exact rules are what make a customer care about your business.
Marketing is not decoration.
Marketing is storytelling.
And most companies are telling the story catastrophically wrong.
So let me give you the single most important idea in this entire book, and if you take nothing else, take this.
The customer is the hero of the story.
Not your brand.
Your brand is not the hero.
The customer is the hero, and you, your company, your product, you are the guide who helps the hero win.
This sounds like a small distinction.
It is not.
It is everything.
Because almost every business instinctively casts itself as the hero.
They talk about their history, their awards, their passion, their founding story, how long they have been in business, how much they care.
They make themselves the star of their own advertising.
And when you position yourself as the hero of the story, you are unconsciously competing with the actual hero, who is the customer.
You are asking the customer to care about your journey, when the customer only woke up this morning caring about their own.
Brands that position themselves as the hero lose.
Brands that position themselves as the guide win.
Think about it in movie terms.
Luke Skywalker is the hero.
Yoda is the guide.
And nobody, not one person, walks out of Star Wars wishing there had been more scenes about Yoda's backstory.
Yoda exists to help Luke.
The moment you understand that your business is Yoda and your customer is Luke, your entire way of talking about yourself has to change.
Now, why does this happen?
Why do good, smart people get this exactly backward?
Because of what Miller calls the two great sins of marketing.
And these two sins explain almost every wasted marketing dollar in the world.
The first sin is failing to focus on the things that help your customer survive and thrive.
Your customer, at a deep and ancient level, is running a survival calculation all day long, whether they know it or not.
Their brain is scanning constantly for anything that helps them conserve resources, save time, save money, gain status, build relationships, stay safe.
If your message does not clearly connect to something in that survival ledger, the brain files it under irrelevant and moves on.

So when a company spends its homepage talking about being founded in 1943, or about its commitment to excellence and synergy, the customer's survival brain hears nothing that matters to it, and it tunes out.
You have to talk about the customer's survival, not your company's story.
The second sin is even more common, and it is the villain of this whole book.
The second sin is forcing your customer to burn too many mental calories to understand you.
Here is the piece of neuroscience the whole book leans on.
The human brain is an energy hog, and it is obsessed with conserving fuel.
Processing information, making sense of things, figuring out what you mean, all of that burns calories, and the brain deeply, instinctively does not want to.
So when a person lands on your website, or reads your brochure, or listens to your pitch, their brain is doing a rapid cost benefit analysis.
It is asking, how much work is it going to take for me to figure out what these people are offering and whether it is worth my time?
And if the answer is that it is going to take a lot of work, the brain does the most natural thing in the world.
It leaves.
Miller has a phrase for this that you should tattoo on the inside of your eyelids.
If you confuse, you lose.
Confusion is not a minor inconvenience in marketing.
Confusion is death.
And here is the line that reframes everything.
People do not buy the best products.
They buy the products they can understand the fastest.
Read that again.
The best product frequently loses to the clearer product.
The company with the superior offering loses to the company with the simpler message.
Because the customer never gets far enough to evaluate the quality.
They bounced off the confusion long before that.
So Miller gives you a beautifully crude test for whether your message is clear enough, and he calls it the grunt test.
Imagine a caveman is sitting in front of your website.
A literal caveman, low on language, thinking mostly in survival terms.
Could that caveman glance at your website for about five seconds and then grunt back to you three things?
Number one, what do you offer?
Number two, how will it make my life better?
And number three, what do I need to do to buy it?
That is it.
What you offer, how it makes life better, and what to do next.
If a caveman can grunt those three things after five seconds on your page, you pass.
If he cannot, you fail, and it does not matter how beautiful the design is or how clever the copy is.
Most websites fail the grunt test spectacularly.
They lead with a vague inspirational tagline, a slideshow of stock photos, a mission statement, and the poor caveman has no idea what is even being sold.
He grunts nothing.
He clicks away.
Keep that caveman in your head for the rest of this book, because he is the merciless judge of everything you will build.
Now, before we get into the framework, I want to make sure you feel in your body why story specifically is the answer, and not just clarity in general.
Because Miller does not say be clear and leave it at that.
He says use the specific machinery of story, and here is why story works.
Miller leans on the great screenwriting teacher Robert McKee, and on a whole tradition of understanding narrative.
A story is fundamentally a device for making sense of the world.
It is how the human brain organizes chaos into meaning.
And every story that has ever held your attention runs on the same engine.
A character wants something, and something is standing in the way of them getting it.
That gap, between what the character wants and their not yet having it, is the thing that grips you.
It is called the narrative gap, or the story gap.
The reason you cannot stop watching is that the gap is open, and your brain hates an open gap.
It wants resolution.
It leans forward.
Here is the crucial and slightly counterintuitive lesson for marketers.
If you close the gap too early, interest dies instantly.
The moment the hero gets what they want, the story is over, and you get up and leave the theater.
So a story survives on tension, on the open question, on the wanting.
And the mistake most brands make with their marketing is that they open no gap at all.
They present no clear want, no clear obstacle, no stakes, nothing for the brain to lean toward.
They just list features into the void.
Story is how you take the noise of your business and organize it into something a human brain will actually follow, because you are working with the oldest attention machinery we have.
So now we get to the heart of the book, the tool that has made StoryBrand famous, and it is a framework of seven parts.
Miller calls it the SB7 framework, which just stands for StoryBrand seven.
And I am going to walk you through all seven, slowly, one at a time, with the examples, so that by the end you can hold the whole thing in your head and reconstruct it from memory.
Because that is the point.
It is meant to be simple enough to carry around.
Here is the whole framework in one breath, the way you would say it as a story.
A character has a problem, and meets a guide, who gives them a plan, and calls them to action, that helps them avoid failure, and ends in success.
Say it again slowly.
A character, has a problem, and meets a guide, who gives them a plan, and calls them to action, that helps them avoid failure, and ends in a success.
Those are the seven parts.
That is the skeleton of essentially every story you have ever loved, and it is about to become the skeleton of your marketing.
Let me take them one at a time.
Part one.
A character who wants something.
The story starts when we meet a hero who wants something.
Not your brand.
The customer.
And your first job is to define, with brutal simplicity, one thing that your customer wants as it relates to your business.
One thing.
Not a list of nine benefits.
One clear desire.
And that desire should connect to survival and thriving in some way, so the survival brain perks up.
A financial advisor's customer wants a comfortable retirement.
A meal kit company's customer wants to feel like a good parent who cooks real dinners.
A lawn care company's customer wants the best looking yard on the block.
Notice how specific and how singular those are.
The mistake companies make here is opening too many story gaps at once.

If your customer wants a comfortable retirement, and also to save on taxes, and also to feel financially literate, and also to leave a legacy, you have opened four gaps, and a brain confronted with four open gaps just closes all of them and walks away.
Pick the one thing.
Define the desire clearly, and you have given the customer's brain something to lean toward.
Part two.
Has a problem.
Now, a hero who wants something but faces no obstacle is boring.
Nobody watches that movie.
It is conflict, it is the problem, that hooks us and makes us care.
And here Miller gives you what I think is the single most commercially useful idea in the whole book, so slow down with me here.
He says that every problem your customer faces actually has three levels, and most companies only ever address one of them, the wrong one, and leave money on the table.
The three levels are the external problem, the internal problem, and the philosophical problem.
The external problem is the tangible, physical, out in the world obstacle.
It is the thing that is literally in the way.
My car is broken.
My house is dirty.
My software keeps crashing.
I do not have a good accountant.
It is concrete and easy to name.
And this is the level almost every company obsesses over, because it is the level their product solves.
But here is the twist.
The external problem is rarely the real reason a person buys.
The external problem is just the ticket into the theater.
The internal problem is how that external problem makes the customer feel.
And this, Miller says, is where the real money is.
This is the sentence to underline in your mind.
Companies tend to sell solutions to external problems, but customers buy solutions to internal problems.
Let me say that again because it is the hinge of the entire book.
Companies sell solutions to external problems, but customers buy solutions to internal problems.
The person whose car is broken does not just have a broken car.
They feel stranded, anxious, incompetent, worried they are going to be taken advantage of by a mechanic.
The internal problem is that anxiety, that feeling of frustration or self doubt or being stuck.
And if your marketing names that internal feeling out loud, the customer feels understood in a way that your competitors, who only ever mention the external problem, never achieve.
When you articulate someone's internal problem, they feel like you can read their mind, and that earns trust at a level that a brand talking only about its features never can.
The philosophical problem is the biggest and deepest level.
It is the why it matters, the sense that it is just plain wrong that this problem exists at all.
It often lives in words like should and should not.
People should not have to feel confused about their own money.
You should not need a computer science degree to build a website.

When you tap the philosophical problem, you elevate your customer's purchase from a transaction into a small act of setting the world right, and human beings are deeply moved by the chance to be part of a story about right and wrong.
Now Miller adds one more crucial move to the problem section, and it is this.
Personify the problem into a villain.
Take the problem and give it a face, a single, relatable antagonist.
Because stories are more gripping when there is a villain, someone or something we can root against.
The villain should be a single identifiable enemy, it should be relatable, so people immediately recognize it, and ideally it should be something you can defeat.
A financial company might name the villain as confusing jargon, or as the sleazy salesman who profits from your ignorance.
An antivirus company personifies the problem as the hacker.
When you name the villain, you give the customer something to be against, and being against a shared enemy bonds them to you as the ally who will help them win.
Miller uses two great examples here that I want you to hold onto.
The first is Tesla, to illustrate the three levels of problem all at once.
Think about a Tesla buyer.
The external problem is simple.
They need a car.
That is the tangible obstacle, they need transportation.
The internal problem is that they want to be an early adopter of good, forward looking technology.
They want to feel like the kind of person who is ahead of the curve, sophisticated, on the right side of the future.
And the philosophical problem is the belief that a person should not have to hurt the environment in order to drive a car.
It is just wrong that getting around should mean polluting the planet.
Now look at what Tesla's marketing does.
It barely talks about the car as mere transportation.
It speaks straight to the internal desire to be an advanced, intelligent early adopter, and to the philosophical conviction that driving should not cost the earth.
They are selling the internal and philosophical solution, and the physical car comes along for the ride.
The second example is CarMax, and it is a beautiful lesson in villains.
CarMax sells used cars.
But think about what the actual pain of buying a used car has always been.
It is not really the car.
It is the experience.

It is the sleazy, high pressure, manipulative used car salesman, the guy who plays games with the price, who makes you feel like you are being cheated, who leaves you anxious that you paid too much for a lemon.
So CarMax named that villain.
The villain is the sleazy used car sales experience itself.
And they built their entire brand as the guide who slays that villain, with no haggle pricing, transparency, no pressure.
They did not really position themselves against other car lots.
They positioned themselves against a feeling, a relatable, universally hated experience, and everyone who had ever felt cheated on a car lot instantly understood exactly what CarMax was for.
Part three.
And meets a guide.
Now we arrive at the reframe I gave you at the very start, made concrete.
The hero is stuck.
The hero has a problem and cannot solve it alone.
If the hero could solve it alone, they would have done it already, and there would be no story.
So into the story walks a guide.
And the guide is you.
Miller says the guide expresses two, and only two, essential things, and if you get these two right, customers trust you.
The two things are empathy and authority.
Empathy comes first, and it comes first for a reason.
Empathy is you saying, in effect, I understand how you feel.
I know what it is like to struggle with this.
Empathy statements often begin with words like, we understand how frustrating it is to, or, we know what it feels like to.
When a customer senses that you genuinely understand their pain, a bond forms.
They stop seeing you as a vendor and start seeing you as someone on their side.

Miller points out that empathy is the thing brands most often skip, rushing straight to how great they are, and it is a fatal skip, because without empathy first, all your credentials just sound like bragging.
Authority is the second thing, and it means competence.
It is you demonstrating that you actually know how to solve this problem, that you have done it before, that you can be trusted to guide.
But, and this is important, Miller says authority should be used sparingly.
A little goes a long way, and too much of it tips you back into being the hero, back into me me me.
You establish authority in a few economical ways.
Testimonials, where your existing happy customers vouch that you got them results, so you do not have to brag about yourself.
Statistics, the hard numbers of how many customers you have served or how much money you have saved them.
Awards, a small, tasteful display of recognition.
And logos, the badges of well known clients or press that you have worked with, which borrow their credibility.
The key word is sparingly.
Just enough authority to be believable, never so much that you become the star.
And now the deepest idea in the whole guide section.
In every great story, the hero is never the strongest character.
Think about it.
Luke is not stronger than Yoda.
Katniss Everdeen is not stronger or wiser than Haymitch, the battle scarred mentor who teaches her how to survive the Hunger Games.
Daniel is not stronger than Mr. Miyagi, who quietly knows a thousand times more about fighting and about life than the boy ever will.
The guide is always the stronger, wiser, more capable character.
And yet the guide is not the hero.
Why?
Because the guide has already had their own adventure, already won their own battle, and has now taken on a new and higher role, which is to serve.
The guide's entire job, the whole reason they exist in the story, is to help the hero win.
This is the posture Miller wants you to adopt as a business.
You may well be the most competent, most experienced, most capable player in your industry.
Good.
But your competence is not there to make you the star.
It is there to serve the hero.
Yoda has all the power, and Yoda spends it entirely on getting Luke ready.
That is your business.
Part four.
Who gives them a plan.
So the hero trusts the guide now.
But trust is not enough to make someone act.
There is still a gap between I trust you and I am going to hand over my money.
And that gap is filled with a very human fear, the fear of the unknown, the fear of making a wrong move, the fear of complexity.
The customer is standing at the edge, thinking, this looks complicated, I do not know exactly what happens next, what if I mess it up.
So the guide gives the hero a plan.
A plan is a clear, simple path, a set of steps that removes the fog and shows the customer exactly how to move forward.
Miller describes two kinds of plans, and you often use both.
The first is the process plan.
This is a short list, ideally three or four steps, no more, that lays out how to do business with you.
Step one, schedule a call.
Step two, we build your custom plan.
Step three, you reach your goal.
Or, step one, order online.
Step two, we ship it to your door.
Step three, enjoy.
The magic of the process plan is that it takes something that felt vague and possibly overwhelming and turns it into three obvious little moves.
It removes confusion about how this actually works.
And notice, fewer steps is better.
If you list nine steps, you have reintroduced the very complexity you were trying to dissolve.
Three or four is the sweet spot.
A person can look at three steps and think, I can do that, that is easy, I see the path.
The second kind is the agreement plan.
This is a list of promises or commitments that you make to the customer, and its job is to remove fear, to dissolve the specific worries that are holding them back.
It is you addressing, out loud, the things that could go wrong in the customer's mind and reassuring them.
We promise our technicians will treat your home with respect.
We promise transparent pricing with no hidden fees.
We promise a satisfaction guarantee.
Each promise defuses an objection before it can stop the sale.
The agreement plan is quiet trust building.
It says, I have anticipated your fears, and here is why you do not need to have them.
Between the process plan and the agreement plan, you have cleared the path and removed the landmines, and the customer can walk forward.
Part five.
And calls them to action.
This is the part where so many otherwise good businesses fail, and it is almost heartbreaking how simple the failure is.
They never clearly ask for the sale.
They build rapport, they show empathy, they explain the plan, and then they get shy, and they never actually say, here is what to do now, buy this.
A hero does not take action unless they are challenged to.
In stories, the hero is passive until something calls them to move.
And your customer is the same.
They will not act unless you clearly, directly, repeatedly call them to.
Miller describes two kinds of calls to action.
The first is the direct call to action.
This is the straightforward ask.
Buy Now.
Schedule a Call.
Register Today.
Get Started.
It is unambiguous, it invites the customer to make the purchase or take the definitive step.
And Miller says you should repeat it shamelessly.
Put it everywhere.
Say it over and over.
And crucially, it belongs in the top right corner of every page of your website.
The top right corner.

That is where the eye has been trained to look for the next move, and there should always be a bright, obvious button sitting there with your direct call to action on it, on the homepage, on the about page, on every page.
Miller argues you should not be timid about this.
A shy business communicates that it does not believe in its own product.
A confident, repeated, clear call to action communicates that you know you can help and you are inviting the customer to let you.
If you are not asking clearly for the sale, over and over, you are quietly telling the customer their problem does not really need solving.
The second kind is the transitional call to action, and this is the more subtle and clever one.
Not everyone is ready to buy today.
Many people are interested but not yet convinced, hovering, thinking about it.
If your only option is buy now, you lose all of those hoverers entirely.
So you offer them a smaller, lower risk step.
A transitional call to action is something you offer, often for free, that lets the customer engage with you without committing to a purchase.
A free guide, a webinar, a free sample, a downloadable checklist, a video series.
Miller describes what this does with a wonderful image.
It stakes a claim on the customer's territory.
When you give someone something genuinely valuable for free, you plant your flag in their world, you become the expert in this domain in their mind, and you create reciprocity.
Reciprocity is the deep human instinct that when someone gives us something, we feel a pull to give back.

So the person who downloads your free, genuinely useful guide feels, at a low quiet level, a growing sense of goodwill and obligation toward you, and when they are finally ready to buy, you are the obvious, trusted choice.
The transitional call to action is how you nurture the not yet ready, so you do not lose everyone who is not buying in this exact moment.
Part six.
That helps them avoid failure.
Now we return to something we touched on with the story gap.
A story needs stakes.
If nothing is at risk, if it does not matter whether the hero succeeds or fails, then we do not care, the tension deflates, and we drift off.
The same is true of your marketing.
You have to gently remind the customer of what is at stake, of what they stand to lose if they do nothing, of the failure they are trying to avoid.
This runs on a deep piece of human psychology called loss aversion.
Human beings are wired to feel the pain of a loss roughly twice as intensely as the pleasure of an equivalent gain.
We are far more motivated to avoid losing something than to gain something of the same size.
So your marketing should paint, at least a little, the negative consequences of not solving the problem.
What does the customer's life look like if they keep going the way they are going?
The money kept wasted, the stress that never lifts, the opportunity that slips away, the dream deferred another year.
But, and Miller is emphatic about this, you must use failure lightly.
Do not drown the customer in doom and fear.
Fear based marketing that lays it on too thick makes people anxious and defensive, and they recoil from you.
The image Miller uses is beautiful and worth keeping.
A little bit of stakes is like salt in a meal.
A pinch of salt makes everything taste better and come alive.
But if you dump the whole shaker into the dish, it is inedible, and you push it away.
So a pinch of failure, a light touch, a gentle reminder of what is at risk, is exactly right.
Just enough for the customer to feel the tension of the open story gap and to understand that the cost of doing nothing is real.
Not so much that you frighten them into paralysis.
Part seven.
And ends in a success.
This is the crescendo, the promise, the reason for the whole journey.
You must show the customer a vision of how good their life will be once they use your product and win.
Never assume people can imagine the happy ending on their own.
You have to paint it for them, vividly and specifically.
Show them the after picture.
If the customer buys, what does their life actually look like?
The clean, gorgeous yard the neighbors envy.
The retirement where they finally feel calm and free.
The confidence of finally understanding their own finances.
Cast a clear, aspirational vision of success, and let the customer see themselves living inside it.
A story that does not resolve leaves us empty.
You have to close the loop and let the hero win, at least in the customer's imagination, so they want to step into it.
Miller then offers a kind of map of what a happy ending even means to human beings, because success is not one flavor.
He describes three broad ways that humans define a satisfying resolution, and your success vision can tap any or all of them.
The first is winning some form of power or status.
The customer becomes more respected, more premium, more capable, elevated in the eyes of others.
This is what a luxury brand or a status product taps.
The second is being unified with something or someone that makes them whole.
This is the resolution of belonging, of connection, of no longer being alone or incomplete.
A dating service, a community, a product that ends isolation, all trade in this.
The third is achieving some form of self realization or self acceptance.
This is the customer becoming the fullest, most authentic version of themselves, reaching their own potential.
A brand that helps someone become fit, or creative, or wise, is offering self realization.
And beneath all three of these sits the deepest layer of all, and it is where Miller ends this part, which is identity transformation.
This is the most profound thing your brand can participate in, so let it land.
Ask yourself, who does your customer want to become?
Not just what do they want to have, but who do they want to be.
Every person is on a journey of becoming someone, an aspirational version of themselves, and the deepest brands understand that they get to play a role in that transformation.
Miller's point is that every brand participates, whether it knows it or not, in the customer's becoming.
The person buying running shoes does not just want shoes, they want to become an athlete, a healthy person, someone disciplined.
The person hiring a financial advisor wants to become the calm, competent steward of their family's future.
When you can name the aspirational identity your customer is reaching for, and position your product as the thing that helps them become that person, you have transcended selling a product.
You have joined the customer's life story at its deepest level.
You are helping them become who they want to be, and there is no more powerful position a brand can occupy.
So that is the whole framework, all seven parts.
Let me say them one more time so they set in your memory like concrete.
A character, who has a problem, meets a guide, who gives them a plan, and calls them to action, that helps them avoid failure, and ends in a success.
Character, problem, guide, plan, call to action, failure, success.
If you can hold those seven in your head, you can diagnose why almost any piece of marketing is working or failing, including your own.
Now Miller gives you some practical tools to actually put this to work, and the first is what he calls the one liner.
The one liner is a couple of sentences that you can say when someone asks what you do, at a party, at a networking event, anywhere.
And it is built from the framework.
It contains the character and their problem, then the plan or the solution, then the success.
So the shape is roughly this.
You start with the problem, most people struggle with such and such.
Then you introduce your offering as the solution.
Then you paint the success.

For example, a landscaper might say, most people are too busy to keep their yard looking good, so we offer full service lawn care that runs on autopilot, which means you get the best looking yard on the block without lifting a finger.
See how that works?
Problem, solution, success, in two breaths.

The one liner is your portable, repeatable story, the thing your employees can say, the thing a happy customer can repeat to a friend, the message that finally travels because it is short enough to carry.
Miller stresses that a one liner is not a slogan you say once.
It is a tool you memorize and repeat until everyone in your company can say it in their sleep.
The second tool is the BrandScript.
The BrandScript is a single page where you write down your answers to all seven parts of the framework.
The one thing your character wants.
Their external, internal, and philosophical problem, and the villain.
How you show up as a guide with empathy and authority.
Your plan, the process and the agreement.
Your calls to action, direct and transitional.
The failure you help them avoid.
And the success you lead them to.
All of it, filled in, on one page.
And then comes the discipline, which is the part people skip and should not.
You test everything against the BrandScript.
Every headline, every email, every ad, every page, every sales conversation.
Does it align with the story on this page?
Is the customer still the hero?
Is the call to action clear?

The BrandScript becomes your filter, the north star that keeps all of your communication coherent and on message, instead of the scattered mess most companies produce where every department invents its own random language.

Now let me walk you through the piece of this book that people find most immediately useful, which is how Miller applies all of this to your website, because he describes an entire wireframe in words, and I want to describe it to you so completely that you could sketch it on a napkin from memory and rebuild your homepage from this audio alone.
Picture a webpage, scrolling from top to bottom, and we are going to walk down it section by section.
At the very top is the header, the part that fills the screen before you scroll, what designers call above the fold.
This is your grunt test zone, the five second zone, the most valuable real estate you own.
It must contain a clear, aspirational promise, a short statement of what you offer and how it makes the customer's life better, and right there, a button.
The promise should be a headline that speaks to what the customer wants, ideally touching that internal problem.
Something like, a smile you will be proud to show off, for a dentist, rather than committed to dental excellence since 1998.
And the button, sitting up in that top right corner as we said, carries your direct call to action.
Above the fold, a caveman must be able to grunt what you sell, how it helps, and what to click.
Nothing else matters as much as this section.
As you scroll down, the next section is a statement of the stakes.
This is part six of the framework made visible, a light touch of what the customer stands to lose.
Just a pinch of salt.
It might say something like, do not spend another year overpaying in taxes, or, the wrong contractor can cost you thousands.
Enough to open the story gap and make them feel the tension, not enough to depress them.
Next comes the value proposition.
Here you lay out the benefits of doing business with you, often as a short list of the good things the customer gets.
This is where you can enumerate a few clear benefits, each one ideally connecting back to how the customer's life gets better, not to your internal features.
Then comes the guide section.
This is where you position yourself as the guide with those two ingredients, empathy and authority.
A line or two of empathy, we understand how stressful it is to, paired with a measured display of authority, your testimonials, a key statistic, a couple of recognizable logos or awards.
This is the section that says, we get you, and we are competent to help you, without ever making yourself the hero.
Below that, you lay out the plan, and Miller specifically recommends showing it as three simple steps.
Three little boxes across the page, numbered one, two, three, each with a short label.
Step one, schedule a call.
Step two, we make a plan.
Step three, you win.
This is the process plan given a visual home, dissolving the customer's fear of complexity right there on the page.
Further down, you include an explanatory paragraph.
This is a longer block of text, more detailed prose, and it serves two audiences at once.

It serves the search engines, because it gives Google real words to read and index so people can find you, and it serves the customer who is a reader, the one who is not going to buy from a headline and a button but wants to dig in and read the fuller story before they commit.

Then Miller recommends a video, if you have one, because video is a powerfully engaging way to tell your story, show your empathy and authority, and let the customer feel your brand as a living thing rather than flat text.
After that, you present your price choices, if it makes sense to show pricing, laying out your options or packages clearly so the customer can see the path to purchase and pick their level.
Clarity here again reduces the mental calories of figuring out what things cost.
And finally, at the very bottom of the page, comes what Miller affectionately calls the junk drawer.
The junk drawer is exactly what it sounds like, the place at the bottom where you put all the links and information that you were tempted to cram up at the top but should not.

Your full navigation, the about page, the careers page, the terms and conditions, the FAQ, the contact details, all the miscellaneous links a complete site needs but that would have cluttered your clean, clear, grunt test passing header.
Everything you wanted to shove into the header out of anxiety goes down here instead, where it is available to the person who is looking for it and invisible to the person who is not.
A great homepage is disciplined at the top and generous at the bottom.
So that is the website.

Header with a promise and a button, stakes, value proposition, the guide with empathy and authority, the three step plan, an explanatory paragraph, a video, price choices, and the junk drawer at the very bottom.
Build it in that order and you have a StoryBrand website.

Miller then extends the framework into two more marketing tools that work together, the lead generator and the automated email sequence, and these are the engine of turning strangers into customers over time.
The lead generator is your transitional call to action made concrete.
It is a short, genuinely useful free resource, usually a downloadable PDF, a checklist, a short guide, that you offer in exchange for an email address.
The key word is genuinely useful.
It has to actually deliver value, solve a small real problem, so that the customer thinks, if the free thing is this good, imagine the paid thing.
When they hand over their email to get it, they have raised their hand, they have stepped from anonymous visitor to known lead, and you have permission to keep talking to them.
And then the automated email sequence is how you keep talking.
Miller describes two kinds of emails working in sequence.
First come the nurture emails, which show up week after week and simply give value.
Not hard selling, just useful, relevant content, tips, insights, small helpful things, that keep you showing up in the customer's inbox as a helpful presence.
And then, woven in, come the sales emails, the ones that make the direct ask, that call the customer to buy.
The reason this works is the compounding effect of showing up in someone's inbox, consistently, over weeks and months, always adding value.
Most customers are not ready to buy the day they meet you.
But if you keep appearing, helpfully, patiently, in their inbox, then when the day comes that they are ready, you are the trusted name that is already there, already established as the guide.
The compounding of small, consistent, valuable touches is what turns a cold email address into a warm customer, and almost nobody does it, which is exactly why it works so well for those who do.
Now I want to give you the case study that Miller uses as the archetype for this whole philosophy, because it makes everything click, and it is Apple.
Think back to the late 1990s.
Apple was nearly bankrupt, a mess of too many products, confusing to explain, dying.
And Steve Jobs returned.
One of the first things Jobs did was radical simplification.
He slashed the product line drastically, cutting it down to a tiny handful of products you could understand at a glance.
But the deeper move, the StoryBrand move, was in how Apple talked about itself.
Before, Apple, like every computer company, talked about megahertz and processor speeds and technical specifications, casting the computer, and by extension the company, as the hero.
After Jobs, Apple stopped talking about processor speeds almost entirely.
Instead, they positioned the creative person, the customer, as the hero.
Think Different was not about the computer.
It was about the crazy ones, the rebels, the creative people who change the world, and Apple was there simply as the tool, the guide, that helps that creative hero do their thing.
And look at how the Apple stores were designed.
They are not full of spec sheets and beige boxes.
They are bright, open, and they show people, images of humans creating and connecting, with the products in a supporting role.
Apple made the customer the hero and made itself the guide, and it became the most valuable company on earth.
That is the entire book, embodied in one company's turnaround.
There is one more application Miller makes that is easy to overlook but genuinely important, and that is turning the framework inward, onto your own company culture.
Because the same clarity that wins customers also aligns employees.
Miller argues that the framework can clarify a company's mission statement just as powerfully as it clarifies external marketing.
And he makes a point that should stop every leader cold.
If your employees cannot repeat what your company does, in clear simple words, then they cannot sell it, and they cannot rally behind it.

A vague, bloated, jargon filled mission statement that no one can remember is not just a communications failure, it is an alignment failure, because a team that cannot articulate what it does cannot pull in the same direction.
When you run your own company through the framework, you give your own people a story they can live inside and repeat.
Clarity is not just how you win customers.
It is how you build a team that knows what game it is playing.
So let me bring this all the way home, because Donald Miller's promise is not abstract.

He tells story after story of small businesses that changed nothing about their product, their pricing, or their operations, and simply changed their words, clarified their message using this framework, and watched their revenue multiply.
Same product.
Same team.
Same market.
New story, told clearly, with the customer as the hero.
That is the whole claim of the book, and it is a genuinely hopeful one, because it means the lever is right there in your hands, in the words you choose.
So let me leave you with the concrete, practical takeaways, the things you can actually do.
First, write your one liner today.
Sit down and craft those two sentences.
The problem your customer has, the solution you provide, and the success they will enjoy.
Get it clear enough that you could say it at a party and the person would immediately understand and even want it.
Memorize it, and teach it to everyone in your company.
Second, audit your website against the grunt test.
Sit a metaphorical caveman in front of your homepage and ask, in five seconds, can he grunt what you offer, how it makes his life better, and what to do to buy.
If not, you have work to do at the top of your page, and nothing matters more.
Third, name your villain.
Sit down and personify the problem your customer faces into a single, relatable antagonist you can position yourself against.
Give the enemy a face.

Fourth, put a button in the top right corner of every page, with a clear, direct call to action on it, Buy Now, Schedule a Call, whatever the definitive step is, and stop being shy about asking for the sale.
Repeat it shamelessly.
And fifth, and maybe most powerful of all, say the internal problem out loud in your headline.
Do not just describe what you sell.

Name how the customer feels, the frustration, the anxiety, the aspiration, right there at the top, so that the customer reads your headline and feels, for the first time, that a company finally understands them.
Because in the end, that is what this whole book is about.
It is about the strange, almost magical power of being understood, and of making other people feel understood.
The customer is the hero.
You are the guide.
Stop confusing people, stop making yourself the star, and start telling a clear story in which the person you serve gets to win.
Clarify your message, and customers will listen.
That is Building a StoryBrand.
